ANIMAL.excerpt: The Good aims at intention, the Beautiful at form, the True at the identity between seeming and essence; Right, however, derives its significance from the relationships, both between God and the creature, and between the creatures mutually.

Since it is now empirically established that such fixed relationships exist; that these inherent relationships do not arise from the creature; that they owe their origin and existence rather to a Will emanating above the creature; and that this Will possesses and exercises the power to maintain what flows from it—thus, Right can only be understood as an order instituted over us by God, which not only regulates our relationship to Him and the mutual relationships between creatures, but also maintains them through punishment and restoration wherever they are violated.
He, however, who lacks the joy of such an all-pervasive faith, can place the source of Law nowhere else than in the creature.
Insofar as this concerns the unconscious creature, one terms these relationships: natural laws.
works purely, while morality spans the entirety of life, encompassing even our relationships to ourselves—to body and soul—and thus also to the animal kingdom, and even to the material realm.

So sharply must one distinguish between the receptivity for the idea of justice, and the content of justice; justice and morality stand so opposed to each other, that justice is the will imposed on us to regulate the relationships in which we stand to God and the creature, while morality is the expression of our own

Let this particular association right now be set aside, and when one comes to the common human right, one must immediately distinguish between the right that God has over us and the right that governs us in relation to the creature, man or beast.
Only the right that regulates the relations between creature and creature forms the subject of our investigation.
THE LEGAL INSTINCT.39 our fellow human beings, animals, and the good things of this earth.
The first form of communication, which regards the sense of right as innate or imprinted, can be conceived of as instinct or as a sum of innate ideas.
The instinct immediately points the human being back to the higher developed animal, since the instinct manifests itself much stronger and in a much more fixed form in all such animals.
Two kinds are to be distinguished in this animal instinct.
There is in the animal a mere technical instinct, which still today evokes our absolute wonder.

Hidden though there may be in our body, from our very first formation, all kinds of technical and chemical processes, and apart from what the human being undergoes unconsciously, or directs unwillingly, like in breathing, his instinct does not make him capable of a single action that would approach the technical perfection even remotely comparable to the spider’s web.
But even the technically precise does not exhaust the instinct in the animal.
The Darwinists try to explain the mysterious that is hidden in this animal instinct by
40 THE LEGAL INSTINCT.
But this does not hold true for the technical instinct.

For while inheritance through custom, which all too often comes down to us from nature, usually has the most precise accuracy, the active instinct, as we observe it especially in the animal world, betrays a far stronger element of a sense of duty, so as not to be conceived of as merely mechanical.

While it can be granted that in mountainous regions the formation of the body exhibits a peculiar habit that the inhabitants of the lowlands lack, and that the child born on the seacoast presents a different appearance in the adult state than the child of the man in the mines, yet in this unmistakable inheritance as a result of custom, there is never a question of identity, as the instinct regularly exhibits.
Inheritance always gives endless variation, instinct the same.
Indeed, with instinct it can be no other than a property implanted in nature.

Just as the heart regulates our pulse without our determining the beat ourselves, and the lungs effect the inhalation and exhalation of air without our controlling the breathing, so also the instinct works in the animal, not according to how the animal thinks, wills and determines it, but mechanically, unfree, and operated by a power that indeed comes to expression in the animal, but has its seat outside the animal.
A similar instinct can also be observed in man, more in the child than in the adult, stronger in primitive peoples than in nations of richer development.

One might even be inclined to regard the instinct in man as little more than a crutch or leading-string to teach him to walk, which would then also fall away of itself once he had finally learned to walk.
THE LEGAL INSTINCT.41 Upon closer examination, however, it appears that those who judge thus are mistaken.

Certainly, much of what was unconscious handling of instinct in the undeveloped person passes over into conscious action in the more mature individual, but it is incorrect to say that this means the instinctive element is entirely eliminated.

473) that instinct is the faculty to do “that which bears the stamp as if it were purposive, while in fact all purposiveness is lacking in the instinctive,” though approaching the truth, still leaves this important element out of account.
First, as a mere technical factor, like in the animal; but then also as a factor of conscious action, which occurs under the impression of a magical urge.
42 THE LEGAL INSTINCT.

ARISTOCRACY.excerpt: To be complete, we would even have to say four, but the “people” as opposed to the aristocracy can remain outside the discussion here.
This was the case with an elite group in every region, but that elite group was far in the minority.

It remains strange, however, that this professor in the first onslaught was always unwilling to speak out that Groen came across so aristocratically, and that it was precisely he who in 1894 sided with the Christian aristocracy to fight the Anti-Revolutionary party, as being too democratically tinted.
One no longer hears at all of the prayer meetings in the salons of our higher aristocracy, where English converts summoned the high-born to conversion.

As for the matter at hand, if one compares the three types of government that philosophers are accustomed to distinguish, I must say that the aristocracy—or a form of government of a mixed nature, where authority is tempered—appears to me by far the most desirable.
What was put forward with regard to Calvin as the contrast between Aristocracy and Democracy, is directly connected with this.
Aristocracy differs in principle from plutocracy.

Now it must not be forgotten that Calvin also adopted the threefold division of monarchy, aristocracy and democracy from Aristotle, and that in the then Greek world the lower classes, who lived by labor, crafts and retail trade, did not even possess civil rights, while the so-called citizens stood on the footing of complete equality.
Aristocracy, Other Than Now.
It is not enough to understand the term “aristocracy” in the sense in which Isaiah and Calvin after him champion it.
ARISTOCRACY, OTHER THAN NOW.

Only this stands irrevocably firm, that Calvin was decidedly opposed to that ancient Democracy, which, in the Greek manner, entrusted the rule, the administration, the government directly to the whole people; for this the nobles were appointed, but by nobles were not understood the rich or eminent, but the most noble, skilled, and talented, the aristocracy in the proper sense.
So also for Languet, aristocracy means nothing other than Polyarchy, and these plures must emerge from the small circle of the morally and intellectually highest and most capable men in the country.

No hereditary power-nobility, nor a money-aristocracy, but a government of multiple persons, and these plures taken from the most excellent sons of the fatherland, regardless of their origins or descent.

For there one reads in Volume VI, page 471, that the Monarchy, if the Ruler aims at his own honor and advantage, necessarily degenerates into Tyranny (degeneration into Tyranny)—just Languet’s proposition; that Aristocracy, i.e.
Aristocracy, Different than Now.
The earlier charters, and in particular the Magna Carta in England, often bore a one-sided character and fitted the condition that gave the aristocracy the upper hand.

Moreover, the traditions in which one lived had borne the stamp of the old saying for too long and too strongly: “As the lords direct, so shall we praise.” Already after the end of the first third of the seventeenth century, the commercial aristocracy had managed to master power, and even in the great cities remained almost nothing to say for our small bourgeoisie, while in the countryside the aristocracy of the Dutch lords still exerted a tighter pressure on the small peasant class.
Yet the Census-conservatism—which one could not call an aristocracy—could not sustain itself.
Already in the ancient Roman Empire, the high aristocracy spoke not Latin, but often Greek, and when now, with the Migration of Peoples, the existing order in the Roman Empire fell apart,

The Church, under the leadership of the Bishop of Rome, first established itself firmly in this, and introduced the use of the Latin language for part of the services and for high assemblies; and as a consequence, the use of the Latin language was soon also introduced for the Respublica litterarum, that is, for what we would call “the learned world.” After a certain historical course, a certain division always arises between the great multitude and a certain elite, which has risen to a higher level through development.
This elite then formed a nobility, an aristocracy, not in blood, but in learning and knowledge.
To have the men ready who must be there when the war breaks out, it is necessary that the repetition exercises provide the elite corps that make a wrestling with large troop masses a reality.

Against the system of repeatedly bringing a branch of administration under European personnel, we therefore place the principle of taking the “Spitze der Behörde,” as our neighbors call it, exclusively from the Mother Country, and of allowing the service to be managed under them by well-educated and strictly disciplined sons of the indigenous aristocracy.

ART.excerpt: The concept of Right stands in the same relation as the equally autonomous concepts of the Good, the Beautiful, and the True.

The Good, as a concept, indicates the character of the moral sphere; the Beautiful, the character of the aesthetic sphere; and the True, that of conformity with the real; and thus, in the concept of Right, is expressed the existence of what ought to be, as opposed to what may not be.
Right, just like the Good, the Beautiful, and the True, is a distinct category of being.

Just as the category of the Good itself creates the antithesis of the non-good, that is, of Evil; just as the category of the Beautiful calls into existence the antithesis of the Ugly; and just as the category of the True is unthinkable without the antithesis of the Lie; so too does the category of that which is Right, by its own motion, place Unright opposite itself as the negation that must be destroyed.

The Good aims at intention, the Beautiful at form, the True at the identity between seeming and essence; Right, however, derives its significance from the relationships, both between God and the creature, and between the creatures mutually.
A picture book, which offers the most beautiful architecture, cannot teach the blind higher architecture, unless first his eyes are opened; something which here of itself comes down to rebirth.
Especially in the field of art, the connoisseur of persons and areas cannot deny inspiration.
There lies in the rich inspiration of many a poet or painter something almost incredible, but the fact is there and is not subject to denial.
Why then what is undeniable in the field of art, could that not also apply to the prophetic inheritance?
We can see, we can hear, we can feel, and so too can we perceive whether something is beautiful or ugly, good or bad, honest or dishonest.
And it is no different with the faculty to have a sensation of the good, the true, and the beautiful, and also of the right.
The weakest and least generally developed is the taste for the beautiful.
In dress and ornaments one constantly sees how the taste for the beautiful is corrupted.
Our taste for the beautiful, and even for what tickles the palate, is just the same.
It is the same with the sensation for the beautiful in the world of tones.
All work reveals its master; and the higher the master stands in his art, the richer is the revelation which he offers in his works.

Now, this technical work of art is accomplished by a newborn spider, completely out of itself; and not even with the front legs, so that one could still think of study, but almost mechanically with the hind legs.
A poet does not bring some rhyming lines together in meter, in order to then compose a poem from these lines.
So a rhymester may tinker, but the poet by God’s grace works entirely the other way around.
With a painter, an architect, a sculptor, a symphony-magician, it is no different.
As far as you have to do with true art, the full image has always been there first, and there was no induction.

Very far behind in the art sense stands the one-year-old child compared to the ten-year-old sparrow or swallow, but even in the child are the beginnings, and from those beginnings the positive and active life of representation develops.

It exists in us humans, without art or study or learning, and it is on the awareness of this, which may be enriched by education but did not arise from it, that all religion, morality, and even legal sensation rests.

The Amun-Chnoubis in Siwa and the Pythia in Delphi may have given a faint afterglow of the working of the magic art, and what we hear of Balaam and the dream-visions of Nebuchadnezzar, may still have stood in very remote connection with the ancient working of the Spirit of God among the nations; but for him who refuses to depart from the firm ground of Holy Scripture, the general revelation in the Word yet closes with the first Patriarch, up to the moment when in the fields of Ephrata the angels’ song resounded of a Peace on earth, and not only for Israel, and thus also of a good pleasure in men and not only in the seed of Abraham; naturally descending upon what we quoted from Zechariah, “the eyes of our God are upon the man,”
The body that we call our own is the product of a higher art, which we did not make ourselves, but received for our use.

Seeing nothing before it but boundless sanding and wilderness in an extensive region that once flourished and bloomed in full natural luxuriance, one who is tender of heart instinctively feels that the productive power of God’s creative art has here been wronged.
It remains, as the poet sang: “Est Deus in nobis, agitante calescimus Illo.” What applies to an orator, applies equally to every other higher art.
And human art in this may be no other than to learn to know it from life itself.

Life has taken on other forms, and forms that are less repulsive; the whole tint of our communal life has changed; and it may be believed that there is a very considerable gain in richer philanthropy and a nobler love of art; but whoever looks up the sin-register of what egoism, what pride, what ambition, what greed, what sensuality, against God’s holy law, has thirsted for, finds it almost no less black, if not blacker than in earlier centuries, and its band is much thicker than the credit of our civic virtue.

Now that Article 25bis of our Morality Laws threatens severe punishment for anyone who makes themselves guilty of that act, and makes no exception for the physician who performs it on good grounds, according to the rules of his art—it is understandable that the physicians have demanded another legal article, so that they would not be placed on the same level as criminals.

Already in the transition from the still entirely formless to the most primordial form, the Idea reveals itself—the Idea of so creating the first matter that the motive is concealed within it to call forth both actual-action and volitional-action in such a determined form.

The human administration of justice, conversely, continues to bear its own distinct type, marked firstly by its very limited scope, secondly by its external form, and thirdly by its coercive character.
All positive law began by appearing in the form of customary law, and even now customary law runs, especially in the East, but still also among us, alongside the law established by statute.
For these forms of life and interaction do not touch the essence, but only the form of society, even though they may have originally arisen from the necessities of life.
“Propriety” is the most perfect form of this noble mode of interaction, and yet even in that propriety one people lags so far behind another.

When Judas in Gethsemane greeted his Master with the kiss of greeting, this was in part, according to the mere outward form, the fulfillment of a social convention then generally in vogue, but the purpose of that kiss was also the betrayal of treason.

They are playthings with legal figures in them, but they have hardly the form in common with the living legal figure in connection with common human life.

It does not see in the air something that has lines, form, and color, and is moving, but it instinctively knows and feels that it is dealing with its enemy.

The image itself of the creature that was to come, stood before the face of God in pure form at once, and the lines, forms, and colors that were combined in it, only served subsidiarily to realize the image seen in the representation.

In an uncorrupted development of our race, these legal instincts would have worked much more powerfully and purely in us, but even in the limited and defective form in which they still operate, they offer a mighty support for society.

must have been present, before the wholly singular atom, the molecule, the electron, or whatever one names the element, attained existence.

Even if one were now to deem it permissible, following the pantheistic fashion, to seek this thinking, willing, and working higher principle within matter itself, the infinite divisibility of matter into its atoms and atomic particles would still point directly to the existence of an independent power, transcending all matter, which intended this composite unity of the infinitely divided, called it into existence as a unity, and held it together in that unity.

Therefore, even if one replaces the successively completed creation, as reported to us in the first chapter of Holy Scripture, with a constantly progressing Evolution from a first singular impulse, one always arrives ultimately at a First Cause, from which all the prerequisites for the origin and continued existence of the universe as a unity, including all its constituent parts, have proceeded.

The sense organ, as we might say, for keen legal feeling can be present in a child still being nourished at its mother’s breast, without any fact, on which that feeling could react, having yet penetrated its consciousness.

Conversely, he who honors the source of all right in God’s decree cannot further analyze this general conviction of faith without also, on his part, reckoning with the nature of man, with the historical course of humanity, and with the philosophical synthesis of the distinct elements of our knowledge.

The attempt to locate the source of Right in man—that is, in man’s nature, in the historical development of humanity, and in man’s philosophical, comprehensive thought—an attempt which, since the Renaissance, broke the continuity of the medieval conception of Right and became increasingly dominant in the so-called “modern” development of jurisprudence, is to be explained by nothing other than a self-deception that constantly shifts the problem.

Both movements could, in the meantime, boast that their leaders had indeed succeeded in bringing the antithesis between the Divine and the human origin of law to a resolution, through the complete exclusion of the Divine.

Both directions—that of Anarchism and that of Russian Nihilism—thus resulted in the downfall of all communal life in both society and state; but it must be recognized that both logically mapped out their course, by carrying the denial of the source of law in God through to the ultimate consequence.

Those, however, who shrank back from the logical consequence of their similar starting point, and maintained the communal life of men—in families and society, in Church and State—as necessary, were unable to maintain the human origin of law otherwise than by leaving deep thought to others, and merely delighting themselves in gazing upon the water’s surface.

POPULAR MORALITY AND CUSTOM.

The custom of greeting one another on the road—and in that greeting making certain accepted movements with the hand—had its origin in the fear that the approaching person could be a robber or a bandit; the failure to greet could then betray this in time to prepare for defense.

When Judas in Gethsemane greeted his Master with the kiss of greeting, this was in part, according to the mere outward form, the fulfillment of a social convention then generally in vogue, but the purpose of that kiss was also the betrayal of treason.

Then indeed the forms of custom and convention would be just as varied as they are now, but would constantly spring from the root of our common human nature in a pure way, and the good in the forms of social intercourse would be just as safeguarded against adulteration by mere appearance.
They point to inherited custom.

For while inheritance through custom, which all too often comes down to us from nature, usually has the most precise accuracy, the active instinct, as we observe it especially in the animal world, betrays a far stronger element of a sense of duty, so as not to be conceived of as merely mechanical.

The ambulance service of the Red Cross has raised the banner of a higher humanity against this raging of bestial passion.
Whoever adheres to the former feeling sees in the State a necessary being that organically arises from the nature of the human race.
National, although it is not a Germanic word, speaks more strongly than popular or folk community the inner feeling.

Just think how, down to the smallest details, in the finest nuances of pronunciation, in the choice of words, and in the binding of conjugation, declension, and construction, the inner feeling, the inner movement of life, and the tenderest transitions of thought find their own peculiar expression in each language—then it seems completely inexplicable how a people who were Finnish at the core of their being could have passed over into a language that owes its origin to a completely different composition of human feelings, perceptions, and thoughts.

In the not too cold, but also not too warm middle region, human nature is characterized by a certain sobriety, self-control, and a strongly emerging willpower; in the hot regions, on the other hand, laxity soon sets in or one has to deal with an overstimulated passion that causes one to flare up one moment and sink into helplessness the next.

In the brother you find, in normal conditions, the majority in physical strength, in intellect, in insight and in willpower; in the sister more of what satisfies the feeling, and what is captivating through tenderness and inner warmth.

But let there be a change, let evil epidemics arise, let the elements strike the people en masse through earthquake, flood, cloudburst, or whatever, or let war threaten and adversity drive the army back, and without distinction you will then observe in all lands and regions an awakening, a becoming alert, of the feeling of religious dependence, and you will read and hear of prayers and offerings, of vows made and promises given, often even of the institution of new forms of worship.

The urge to do so is so overpowering that more than once a Minister of the Crown, of whom everyone knew that he had allowed the sense of atheism to dull his religious feeling, yet felt that he could not and dared not omit it, while the Bearers of the Crown were themselves so strongly convinced of this, that in all the years of our kingship not a single legislative proposal has ever been submitted to the States-General otherwise than with this final formula: “And herewith, gentlemen, we commend you to God’s holy keeping.” And although it must be acknowledged that in 1813 the obligation to include the formula “By the grace of God” in the title of the law was omitted, the fact remains nevertheless that even from the 6th of December 1813, the decree of the Sovereign Prince began with these words: “We, William, by the grace of God, Prince of Orange-Nassau, Sovereign Prince of the United Netherlands,” etc., etc., and that since 1813 not a single decree or law has ever come into force, or the same derivation of the Supreme Authority has been repeated therein, and this under the countersignature of all ministers without distinction, who thus by their co-signature also took this part of the content upon their own account.

However the egoist may rise up in resistance against this binding together of persons, and for the sake of that egoism, try to pulverize the whole into individual particles, as if they were self-subsistent parts, the feeling of fellowship keeps constantly reviving in every possible way.

Until that time, the Holy Scripture cuts off all possibility, as if the eternal peace of a united human race could never be heralded by ready development, and alongside this testimony of the Holy Scripture, the testimony of experience also steps forward, that the prophetic word of Scripture impresses its equal seal.

For the State, too, the essence lies in the people, but that which makes a people into a State is neither eternal, nor necessary, nor unchangeable, nor inherent in the nature of the people.
The State is to them nothing more than a particular, not eternal and unchangeable form in which the being of the people manifests itself.

And alongside this impressive reality runs Cosmic Pacifism, which not only wants to ban war and guarantee eternal world peace, but in highly excited fantasy reaches much further, and now already on Peace Conferences, through the Court of Arbitration, and so much more, thinks to encompass the whole world and dreams of being able to call into being a world-encompassing world confederation or league of states, and has even already devised plans to abolish all fleets and armies and to leave only a small, central military force at the disposal of the world government, in order, if necessary, to compel a rebellious group or a quarrelsome people by force to submit.
We too believe in a world empire, and we too believe in eternal peace, but never otherwise than as the fruit of the Cross.
And then the kingdom of eternal peace will enter of itself.

We too believe in a world empire, and we too believe in eternal peace, but never otherwise than as the fruit of the Cross.
And then the kingdom of eternal peace will enter of itself.
It is not otherwise in the world of the stars, and it will soon become apparent in eternity that it is not otherwise even in the world of angels.

This doubt, however, is dispelled not only for those who hold fast to Scripture, by the account of Paradise and the prophecy concerning the eternal Kingdom of Christ, but also for those who reject Scripture, by the simple fact that all human types can sexually intermix and produce offspring—something that would be impossible if they were truly distinct species.

But with this recognition, one also immediately finds oneself confronted with the insoluble problem of how both can be true at the same time: that on the one hand God’s Providence has ordained everything from eternity, and how a desire arising in the heart of God’s child and presented to God in prayer can still lead to the realization of the purpose intended in the prayer.
He who stands for the abolition of armies and navies, for eternal peace, for having done once and for all with all strife and war, cannot believe this of himself.
He does indeed thirst for the eternal peace, but he cannot expect that except with the return of Christ.

In the removal of all such dangerous and easily inflammable material, therefore, there is gain, and although it stands firm for us that the “eternal peace” cannot be ushered in before the return of Christ, and although it cannot be denied that whoever believes that is indulging in utopias, our love for peace must never be silenced by military inclinations.
We therefore support the Pacifists in every effort to ward off war as long as it is at all possible, but we do not delude ourselves that peace will be eternal.
The second attempts to create a power to which all peoples will voluntarily submit, in order to thus prepare the eternal world peace.

In connection with this, the question now requires an answer as to whether what the Pacifists express with their “League of Nations” can indeed lead to the final banishment of war and the emergence of an eternal peace.

EVOLUTION.excerpt: Yet it was clear to me from the outset that, given a conceivable further development of the significance of our party in the Dutch political life, a more complete and more systematic exposition of what the Antirevolutionary party professed and aimed for in this country could not be avoided.

Without the slightest claim to anything lying outside my intellectual sphere, and without competing even remotely for a seat of honor in the company of these political heroes, I aim solely, through this “Further Elucidation,” to render an account of my political insight, both in its initial formation and in its later development, so that, without any higher pretension, I may thereby strengthen only those who feel spiritually akin to me in their conviction.

For him, then, above the obscure reasoning of the solitary thinker stands thinking humanity in its historical development; and above the thinking of ordinary humanity stands the thinking of those who proved gifted with legal genius.

history and man’s philosophy must always explain whence man received such a nature, how he brought it to such an expression in his historical development, and what enabled him to achieve a comprehensive understanding of it.

The attempt to locate the source of Right in man—that is, in man’s nature, in the historical development of humanity, and in man’s philosophical, comprehensive thought—an attempt which, since the Renaissance, broke the continuity of the medieval conception of Right and became increasingly dominant in the so-called “modern” development of jurisprudence, is to be explained by nothing other than a self-deception that constantly shifts the problem.

Especially those who allowed themselves to be carried away by the Idea of Evolution in the previous century, believed they could suffice by employing three sorts of data—which they found in man’s nature, history, and philosophy—as the granite and hard stone for their construction of law, and further imagined they could dispense with all closer investigation concerning the quarry from which these blocks were hewn.

For, based on the Theory of Evolution, it was now established that in the analysis of any life whatsoever—thus also of human life—one ultimately descended to life-germs arising from virtually nothing, to which one could then attribute everything requiring explanation

Now, this theory of Evolution, thus conceived, is one to which, quite wrongly—because it contained a few data that commended it—a nearly universal validity was ascribed for a time; an estimation which has already partly lapsed.
Yet in no case does the Theory of Evolution offer a solution here.

Therefore, even if one replaces the successively completed creation, as reported to us in the first chapter of Holy Scripture, with a constantly progressing Evolution from a first singular impulse, one always arrives ultimately at a First Cause, from which all the prerequisites for the origin and continued existence of the universe as a unity, including all its constituent parts, have proceeded.

And what is no less worthy of note: the absence of the sinful element, due to the non-occurrence of the Fall in Paradise, would by no means have excluded a gradual growth through education and development, and an ascent in the degree of both the consciousness of right and the consciousness of morality.
Such a natural development would have had nothing in common with sin, in whatever sense.
The assurance that right is secured in the further development of life.
A similar instinct can also be observed in man, more in the child than in the adult, stronger in primitive peoples than in nations of richer development.
The richer the consciousness, self-control and unfolding of power, the less trace of instinctive operation remains to be discovered

In an uncorrupted development of our race, these legal instincts would have worked much more powerfully and purely in us, but even in the limited and defective form in which they still operate, they offer a mighty support for society.

Here we are immediately confronted with the decisive dilemma, whether according to the theory of evolution, man has been refined from the animal, or whether he has been created directly as a product of the Divine invention in his own kind.

Let us not forget that even before Darwin, when the theory of evolution had not yet come, the less far-reaching representation was already in vogue, as if man, having come into the world as a half-barbarian, had only gradually become civilized and humane, something which in the days of the French Revolution was again disputed by reference to the inhabitants of the South Sea islands and by the raving about the state of innocence of nature—something that especially drove the Aboriginal Society in London, but these small variations need not be noted here.

The contrast that is at stake here has been carried out to its strictest consequence in Darwinism, and in the twentieth century the problem can no longer be posed otherwise than as it has been placed by the theory of evolution against the doctrine of Genesis.

Now, since on the basis of the statements of Christ, to which we have referred, no Christian jurist can even doubt that the theory of evolution must, in what it presumed, be rejected, so that we can only depart from the creation story in the Mosaic documents, the question arises whether there has been a direct legal imposition by the word, or whether commands, precepts or dispositions from a Higher Hand have been imposed on Adam and Eve and their immediate descendants.
Of course, according to the theory of Evolution, there was no question of this among the first humans.

If the animal world is not a product of evolution, but rather in its entire structure, and thus also in its internal organization, has come into being through the sovereign Almighty God, then it necessarily follows that this Almighty God must also have been able to subject both the structure and the organization to a decisive change, so that the organization, which was first aimed at peaceful coexistence, was now directed towards mutual destruction.
THE LEGAL GENIUS OF PERSONS AND PEOPLES.73 More precise development, and the knowledge gained thereby, naturally works back on the legal order in smaller and lower circles.

To discover how God has fixed all these relationships in life, and this with inclusion of all the variations that the multiplicity of all organic life brings with it, and with all the modifications that the development of these relationships necessitates through sin—this is the sacred task of all legislation, jurisprudence, and legal study.
What presents itself as abnormal is then conceived of as the natural fruit of still incomplete development, a view that of itself completely pushes aside the concept of punishment.

It is as if the perfection will be able to enter through the very development of the idea of the State.

The champions of the Rechtsstaat did not take it this far, but the principle of their contention also remains that it is the State which, by establishing the supremacy of Law—inwardly in the development of the people, and outwardly in the organization of the state—will bring us to a future in which humanity will gradually attain the realization of its ideal.

Not as if from that sense the knowledge of the law could be directly derived, but in the sense that man, the higher his development, the more clearly he is imbued with the conviction that he, and with him all men, are subject to a higher legal order, and that only by the coming to dominance of that higher legal order, the happiness of human society can be promoted.

There is naturally in us not only the possibility to feel the struggle between what is right and what is wrong, but also the starting points for the law itself, which are innate in us, implanted in us, and which are not first impressed upon us by experience or education.
In this state of affairs, history often renders us the desired service of showing us from experience how far the elsewhere taken trial has succeeded, and in what way it has pressed for improvement.

To take this knowledge into ourselves is only possible for us insofar as God has endowed us with the sense to feel what is right; and after having armed us with this sense, He has furthermore given us a legal consciousness through instinct and perception, laying bare certain fundamental principles of law for all humanity in the first nine chapters of Genesis, and indeed through what God Himself spoke in words; and after thus having oriented us and made us capable of observation, He reveals to us the legal relationships ordained by Him in the very fabric of life and in history; causes the leaders of peoples to grasp the law in practical wisdom; illuminates this practical insight by raising up legal geniuses among the nations, or by bestowing a special legal sense on certain peoples; and finally evokes lawmakers who, by their authority, establish the well-trodden conceptions of jurisprudence.

Even if one imagines that the confederate commonwealth of all states could become our future, one still has to ask oneself whether experience has indeed taught that this would once and for all put an end to fighting and striking with the sword.

Where I would admit that among us Anti-Revolutionaries after 1890, 1904, and 1913—that is, after the years in which three Right-wing cabinets under Mackay, Kuyper, and Heemskerk took office—the interest in devotion to the Anti-Revolutionary party began to compete all too noticeably with the love for our principle, the exclusion that the Liberal monopoly had caused us to experience has never been forgotten.

And although a certain weakening of principles has now also crept in among us, and although one encounters a certain subordination of the political to the economic life even in the younger generation, which did not experience the years of need from ‘57 to ‘87, yet the conclusion to which the first part of our investigation led still holds true.

Against the completely false notion that man, with the help of his powers of observation and conceptual gymnastics, could invent the absolute, uniquely valid state system, and that henceforth in all lands, century after century, no other system than this unique system should be introduced, the party now chose the slogan of the historical, and likewise protested against the equally unjust idea that, in sailing on the stream of history, all use of a compass was superfluous, by the addition of “Christian” before “Historical.” In the term “Historical,” it was recognized that the system according to which the state was to be directed did not offer a single system for all lands and all ages, but that, even where the deepest principles remained the same, the forms of the system varied according to the circumstances of countries and times.
This power partly arose from our human mode of being, partly from the facts of experience, and partly from the brains of thinkers.

FAMILY.excerpt: There is not a single people that has not seen objectionable customs creep into its sphere of life—such as, for example, the condoning of lying, pregnancy before marriage, drunkenness, etc.

And further, the mutual greeting on birthdays or name-days, the announcement of an intended marriage, at a birth or a death, the visits to the newborn or the bereaved, the accompaniment at a funeral, the regular visiting of one another, the offering of small gifts, the leaving of calling cards, the appearing in festive attire at high celebrations, the wishing of a new year, the rising when a woman enters, the going before her, the honoring of good customs in decent and disciplined clothing, the not interrupting someone before they have finished speaking, the referring to oneself as a willing servant, the addressing in writing with an ascribed title, the use of standardized terms of address on the envelope—the rising and stepping aside of the younger—all these are customs and social forms that, without legal or ethical character, nevertheless shape the outward appearance of our social life.
Another legal provision that is established is that of marriage.
Secondly, the marriage law itself is contained herein.
In a later life that unfolds anew, there is, according to Jesus’ statement, no longer any question of a marriage bond (Matt.
If in case of illness, childbirth, death, or even in the ordinary household, help from third parties is needed, a relationship arises here too between those serving and the household, under the head.
The regulating authority naturally emanates from the head of the household.

Quite spontaneously, the family emerges in this way, and as long as one has not been seduced by fantasies, such as Plato advocated as the highest wisdom, the family formation governs the whole of society, and thus, in a normal state of affairs, the legal relationship between man and woman, parents and children, brothers and sisters, free and servants, household members and strangers regulates itself.
FAMILY, CLAN, NEIGHBORHOOD.67 For these legal relationships, no legal order is proclaimed, but in the normal course of things, it all regulates itself unnoticed and naturally.
And if there are quarrels, the head of the household settles the matter.
The head of the household also bears responsibility for this, but his right to dispose of everyone’s services and assistance makes this possible.
Family, Clan, Neighborhood.
The first question arises regarding the further regulation of the members who leave the family and form their own household, and then their coming into contact with neighbors.
From the family comes the clan.

The patriarchal communal life that arose from this brought with it its own legal order, and although the sense of rights and duties that still resonates in connection with this is by no means what it once was, the rights and obligations of this family bond still work among us.
This comes out most clearly in the disputes that arise with the neighboring family groups of a more fixed formation, as one still finds, for example, among the Berbers in Algeria and Morocco.
68 FAMILY, CLAN, NEIGHBORHOOD.
The head of the family or clan could not, without the right to summon the aid of others in the event of an external attack, bear the responsibility for the family or clan.
FAMILY, CLAN, NEIGHBORHOOD.69 When these are grasped together, life itself is again at stake—what help one owes another, and how to order rights and duties.
In direct connection with these rights and obligations arising from the family, the clan, and the neighborhood, stood the legal claims relating to movable and immovable property.
And in addition to this, there is in the second place the movable property of cattle, tents or houses, clothing, household goods or tent equipment, weapons, toys, ornaments, etc.

In marriage with a young woman from another place, in the joint use of a well, in the division of the harvest, in the distribution of the spoils of the hunt—everywhere the need for regulation arises, not first from agreement, but from the very nature of life itself.

The giving of marriage gifts, or the granting of ransoms, in the disposition of goods left by the deceased, in buying and selling, in renting and leasing, in traffic on water or whatever it may be—again and again the very substance of the living desire of the one collides with the desire of the other.
The same distinction applies here as between ordinary family life and the household life in a colossal hotel, in a large institution, or in a gigantic barracks,
You will almost never find a written, let alone printed, household order in a family.
A respectable head of household, a decent housewife regulates and rules it all with a glance of the eye and a brief word, and on that footing all human association has begun in the small.
One still speaks of the state of death, of a defective state, of the state of marriage, etc., in exactly the same sense.

If there were no more disruption in nature as a consequence of the curse, the struggle against nature and the correction of nature would be no longer necessary; and all that is now regulated and ordered by the government would find all the necessary provisions within the organic family, clan, tribe, and national relationships.
This feeling of connection and solidarity does not confine itself to family life, but extends time and again to ever-wider circles.
From the family to the clan, from the clan to the tribe, from the tribe to a people-whole that forms itself either spontaneously or by choice.

The antipathy against Calvinism, which Groen van Prinsterer also recognized, was at the time mainly based on the doctrine of Predestination.
1 of the Regulations, the doctrine of Predestination, which had always been the cor ecclesiae, i.e.

More clearly than anything else, the dogmatic article of this regulation, which was drawn up in a Methodist manner, shows how Groen van Prinsterer at the time did not even have the doctrine of Predestination incorporated into his own spiritual conception, yes, he was still very doubtful about it.
Groen’s merit now precisely consisted in this, that in spite of his scruples against Predestination, he gradually gained an open eye for the high merit of Calvinism as a political phenomenon.

The reason for this lies in the undeniable fact that Calvin was primarily a theologian; that in Calvin’s theology, predestination not only stood in the foreground, but dominated his entire system; and that for this reason the very name of Calvinism sounds to them nothing other than an exclusively theological term.

Antirevolutionary is a name of political significance for them, and so is the designation Christian-Historical; but Calvinist is a name to which they almost exclusively attach a theological meaning, and then specifically as a term referring to the dogma of predestination, which remains unusable for their ethical or Methodistic sensibilities.

It is therefore absolutely necessary that it be once and for all declared that to be a Calvinist in the political sphere in no way implies the requirement to be a proponent of the dogma of predestination.
This would only be the case if the political direction in which the Antirevolutionaries move were determined primarily by the dogma of predestination.

Our conclusion is therefore: first, that the slogan: Against the Revolution, the Gospel, by no means condemns any popular uprising or political revolution, but only turns against the principled French Revolution of 1789, and this not because of the actual events that took place, but because of the political doctrine which, after having been recommended from various quarters for three centuries, was then officially introduced for the first time, and was imposed as the way to the happiness of all nations.

Groen van Prinsterer now placed the Gospel against this, not as if the Gospel offered us an elaborated political program, but to confess that it brings no happiness and salvation to our human life, that it is not the political program of Voltaire, but the life program that Jesus Christ gave us in the Gospel.
It would therefore be a cause of joy to me if I could put an end to the confusion that now reigns.

The attempt to locate the source of Right in man—that is, in man’s nature, in the historical development of humanity, and in man’s philosophical, comprehensive thought—an attempt which, since the Renaissance, broke the continuity of the medieval conception of Right and became increasingly dominant in the so-called “modern” development of jurisprudence, is to be explained by nothing other than a self-deception that constantly shifts the problem.

Especially those who allowed themselves to be carried away by the Idea of Evolution in the previous century, believed they could suffice by employing three sorts of data—which they found in man’s nature, history, and philosophy—as the granite and hard stone for their construction of law, and further imagined they could dispense with all closer investigation concerning the quarry from which these blocks were hewn.

If we observe, however, from the history of all ages and among all peoples, that the religious sensation of faith is also found in the spiritual life of man, then it appears that complete harmony exists between this religious awareness and the logical result found; and the adoring veneration of the Cause of all things—concerning both their origin, existence, and coherence—in God Almighty, is justified from every perspective, providing precisely what is required for the explanation of the ultimate ground of all existence.

But even though under many flaws and many temptations to sin, the Common Grace has also here worked to avert corruption, and has enriched us in the course of history with a culture that ever more nobly unfolds.
Justice is the historical, morality the eternal.
Not from the Bible, but from nature, history, and the science of the peoples outside Israel, must this enthronement of the law be known.
But history is not a clock that, once wound up by God, now runs of itself to its conclusion.
That the particular emerges here and there in this history is undeniable.

Time and again, history tells us of extraordinary personalities and extraordinary events that brought about a mighty turning point in existence; and in all this too there is revelation; but always in such a way and to such a degree that the personal and the natural are not transcended, and that there is no question of a Special Revelation.

Yet the concept of Right did not arise merely as an antithesis.
The concept of Right stands in the same relation as the equally autonomous concepts of the Good, the Beautiful, and the True.

THE CONCEPT OF RIGHT.

447, brings us much closer to the truth by pointing out that our *Recht* is connected to the Latin *Rectus*, derived from *regere*, and that it thus contains the idea of ruling—that is, ordering, regulating, and governing; an interpretation confirmed by the word ‘right hand,’ as the opposite of that which is left, something which likewise aims at dominion, e.g., in the saying that Christ is seated “at the right hand of His Father.” The derivation of the word ‘Judge’ also points to a similar significance.
Right is the outflow of a power that establishes or restores a certain order, and its antithesis lies in what is crooked, in what is left, in what is confused, in what is chaotic.
Yet the concept of Right did not arise merely as an antithesis.
The concept of Right stands in the same relation as the equally autonomous concepts of the Good, the Beautiful, and the True.

The Good, as a concept, indicates the character of the moral sphere; the Beautiful, the character of the aesthetic sphere; and the True, that of conformity with the real; and thus, in the concept of Right, is expressed the existence of what ought to be, as opposed to what may not be.
Right, just like the Good, the Beautiful, and the True, is a distinct category of being.

Just as the category of the Good itself creates the antithesis of the non-good, that is, of Evil; just as the category of the Beautiful calls into existence the antithesis of the Ugly; and just as the category of the True is unthinkable without the antithesis of the Lie; so too does the category of that which is Right, by its own motion, place Unright opposite itself as the negation that must be destroyed.

The Good aims at intention, the Beautiful at form, the True at the identity between seeming and essence; Right, however, derives its significance from the relationships, both between God and the creature, and between the creatures mutually.

Since it is now empirically established that such fixed relationships exist; that these inherent relationships do not arise from the creature; that they owe their origin and existence rather to a Will emanating above the creature; and that this Will possesses and exercises the power to maintain what flows from it—thus, Right can only be understood as an order instituted over us by God, which not only regulates our relationship to Him and the mutual relationships between creatures, but also maintains them through punishment and restoration wherever they are violated.
All this would pass outside our consciousness if the spiritual feeling for the antithesis between Right and Unright were not given in our mentality.
And in an entirely similar sense, separated from the question of what is substantively right or unright, there is in our mentality a spiritual sense that reacts to the Category of Right or Unright.
This is our innate or implanted consciousness of Right or Unright, which, among all four mentioned, manifests itself most strongly in reaction to our perceptions.
Thus it appears that, entirely apart from any specific content of the Right, we are susceptible to a very strong sensation of right or unright.
It is inherent to man, apart from all content, and thus also apart from the question of whether we are actually right.
Someone who is completely mistaken in the conception of his right, and regards as unright what justly befalls him, often reacts to this false sensation of un
THE SOURCE OF RIGHT.
right often just as strongly, sometimes even more strongly, than he who is actually right.
If it is now established herewith that there is in us a *consciousness* of Right or Unright, the question now arises: how do we come to know the content of the Right?
The Source of Right.

If by knowledge of the Right is meant the knowledge of what is considered valid law in a specific country, in a specific year, with respect to a specific case, then the direct source from which this knowledge flows is the written law, as known from the Constitution, statute, decree, or ordinance; at least in part, not infrequently in connection with a certain *customary law* (gewoonte-recht).
However, even this positive law cannot always be known from its sources in a manner impervious to contradiction.

Right, taken in this sense—as a regulation of relationships established by God, in which conscious creatures ought to stand and interact with Him and mutually with one another—is thus a disposition proceeding entirely independently from God alone, the knowledge of which can only come to us out of or through Him.
Travelers, who have enriched us with knowledge of foreign national conditions, were accustomed indeed to speak of popular morality in a more general sense, but this was not accurate.
Now, it must be more closely investigated how God brings us the sense and knowledge of law.

However much here, for our purpose, the renewal of the lost knowledge of the law comes to the fore, yet it needs no further argument that with the knowledge of the law, an imprinting in the memory is not enough.
Legal knowledge without restored legal consciousness may enrich the memory—deepen and clarify it—but it cannot elevate your legal awareness.
Here too the assertion returns that it is God who reveals to us the lost knowledge of the law, but on the condition that this happens exclusively in a completely natural way.

The regeneration of each individual person, and the Incarnation of the Son of God for our entire race, combined with prophecy and direct revelation, remain for us the indispensable extraordinary data that can lead us to the knowledge of the law as it is in God and comes from God over us.
Yet he is completely mistaken who, placing himself on this standpoint, imagines that we have nothing else to do in order to arrive at the knowledge of the law than to open up the Holy Scriptures.
What this Revelation bestows upon us always connects to the existing, and therefore the starting point for the knowledge of the whole can never lie elsewhere than in the existing.
The very same is observed in the moral sphere and in the realm of truth.

Quite apart from the question whether there is an innate legal knowledge in us, it is the sense of justice implanted in us and maintained against all corruption, which has made an ordered society among men possible, and it cannot be praised highly enough that the danger that arose from the Fall against this has been averted by Common Grace.
Not from the Bible, but from nature, history, and the science of the peoples outside Israel, must this enthronement of the law be known.

The knowledge of the right can be thought of: 1° as innate to us by nature; 2° as communicated to us, i.e., by word, dream, or image; and 3° as something for us to deduce from the data that nature and history set before us.
Only where the spiritual sense for the perception of right is present and active can knowledge of the right be acquired.
Wisdom in distinction from intellectual knowledge, tact in conduct, the ability to make a fortunate grasp, even genius—all these are akin to the instinctive.

The dove does not arrive at this knowledge through reasoning, nor through what it has learned in ornithology, for even if it has never before seen such a bird of prey, it still knows instinctively that it is dealing with its enemy.

Only when my defective hearing increasingly confined me to the study could time be won for this; and it is this ampler disposal of my time that has enabled me to complete the work now appearing.
I would have preferred to retain the simple title of “Further Elucidation” for both parts that this work will comprise, just as the first installment appeared under that simple title.

Even within a country, a similar process has been at work.
Just as every sensation to which we are susceptible can work weakly or strongly, so it is with our sense of justice.

In connection with this, Professor Redslob in his work Abhängige Länder (Leipzig 1914) only recently dealt with the peculiar position in which an ever wider group of countries has come, through the fact that they have lost their statehood.

There is indeed a will at work, but it is not yet a unity of will for the whole of our human race together.

To let the sense of justice work more intensively on consciousness and practice is a step forward on the long road that must lead to the triumph of the truly human in our society over the all too bestial.

LAW.excerpt: Upon much good counsel, I therefore adopted the title “Antirevolutionary Politics.” Not “Antirevolutionary Constitutional Law.” The expression Constitutional Law would have been too comprehensive a concept, one that would have required, first, an exposition of the general system of all Antirevolutionary politics, and only thereafter a separate demonstration of the widely divergent forms that this general system has assumed according to differences in countries and times.
The ethnological-historical limitation is self-evident not in the concept of Constitutional Law, but indeed in the concept of Politics.

I am not a Jurist, neither by disposition nor by study, and the double doctorate in Political Science and in Law, which was granted to me *honoris causa*—the first from Princeton and the second from Louvain—has not metamorphosed me into one.
Our starting point must be the establishment of Law.

Not as if, since Rudolph Gneist advanced the concept of the “Rule of Law State” (*Rechtstaat*), the life of the state could be confined within the legal sphere—and even though criticism of this representation remains a duty—yet, whoever on his part wishes to give direction to the political life among his own people cannot escape the demand to explain the meaning and significance of Law.

THE CONCEPT OF RIGHT.

447, brings us much closer to the truth by pointing out that our *Recht* is connected to the Latin *Rectus*, derived from *regere*, and that it thus contains the idea of ruling—that is, ordering, regulating, and governing; an interpretation confirmed by the word ‘right hand,’ as the opposite of that which is left, something which likewise aims at dominion, e.g., in the saying that Christ is seated “at the right hand of His Father.” The derivation of the word ‘Judge’ also points to a similar significance.
Right is the outflow of a power that establishes or restores a certain order, and its antithesis lies in what is crooked, in what is left, in what is confused, in what is chaotic.
Yet the concept of Right did not arise merely as an antithesis.
The concept of Right stands in the same relation as the equally autonomous concepts of the Good, the Beautiful, and the True.

The Good, as a concept, indicates the character of the moral sphere; the Beautiful, the character of the aesthetic sphere; and the True, that of conformity with the real; and thus, in the concept of Right, is expressed the existence of what ought to be, as opposed to what may not be.
Right, just like the Good, the Beautiful, and the True, is a distinct category of being.

Just as the category of the Good itself creates the antithesis of the non-good, that is, of Evil; just as the category of the Beautiful calls into existence the antithesis of the Ugly; and just as the category of the True is unthinkable without the antithesis of the Lie; so too does the category of that which is Right, by its own motion, place Unright opposite itself as the negation that must be destroyed.

The Good aims at intention, the Beautiful at form, the True at the identity between seeming and essence; Right, however, derives its significance from the relationships, both between God and the creature, and between the creatures mutually.

Since it is now empirically established that such fixed relationships exist; that these inherent relationships do not arise from the creature; that they owe their origin and existence rather to a Will emanating above the creature; and that this Will possesses and exercises the power to maintain what flows from it—thus, Right can only be understood as an order instituted over us by God, which not only regulates our relationship to Him and the mutual relationships between creatures, but also maintains them through punishment and restoration wherever they are violated.
All this would pass outside our consciousness if the spiritual feeling for the antithesis between Right and Unright were not given in our mentality.
And in an entirely similar sense, separated from the question of what is substantively right or unright, there is in our mentality a spiritual sense that reacts to the Category of Right or Unright.
This is our innate or implanted consciousness of Right or Unright, which, among all four mentioned, manifests itself most strongly in reaction to our perceptions.
Thus it appears that, entirely apart from any specific content of the Right, we are susceptible to a very strong sensation of right or unright.
It is inherent to man, apart from all content, and thus also apart from the question of whether we are actually right.
Someone who is completely mistaken in the conception of his right, and regards as unright what justly befalls him, often reacts to this false sensation of un
THE SOURCE OF RIGHT.
right often just as strongly, sometimes even more strongly, than he who is actually right.
If it is now established herewith that there is in us a *consciousness* of Right or Unright, the question now arises: how do we come to know the content of the Right?
The Source of Right.

If by knowledge of the Right is meant the knowledge of what is considered valid law in a specific country, in a specific year, with respect to a specific case, then the direct source from which this knowledge flows is the written law, as known from the Constitution, statute, decree, or ordinance; at least in part, not infrequently in connection with a certain *customary law* (gewoonte-recht).
However, even this positive law cannot always be known from its sources in a manner impervious to contradiction.

When in the middle of the 19th century, as a result of the steam engine, the progress of technology, the easier transportation, a tenfold increase in credit, and a strong increase in the population, all personal independence of position was lost for many in the working class, and the individual was brought under the overwhelming power of capital, the dominant group has often known how to push through legal provisions that disrupted the balance, and refused to elevate into law provisions that were indispensable for the wage-earning population to maintain their personal independence and viability.
There was certainly a concept of partial independence expressed, almost always in relation to equal powers of other estates or groups.

This suction towards union works so strongly that the individual person constantly feels the danger of losing their independence, their self, their individuality in the too strong current.
Hence the reaction against this suction in the drive for freedom.

Greed and lust for power have led one people to deprive another of its independence, and no less have power-hungry tyrants risen up to bring pieces and parts of all kinds of ethnic groups under their own scepter, and then to combine these heterogeneous elements into a separate union.
That this has not happened with our Dutch is a strong argument for the independence of the Dutch people in the various provinces, and not a little strengthens the unity of our state as a whole.

As a rule, this happens on a very small scale through constant naturalization, but it can also follow on a larger scale, either that one people overwhelms the other, deprives it of its independence, and absorbs it into itself and assimilates it, as the Celts did to the Basques, and the Romans and Germans did to the Celts.
The sea is the great teacher of the nations, awakening their sense of adventure, their spirit of enterprise, their love of freedom.
It teaches seafaring, and thereby increases the zest for life and the energy, the sense of independence and the willpower to dare.

Given this state of affairs, Pan-Germanism now finds the disproportionate disparity that the small Netherlands thrives and flourishes in the possession of its coasts, seaports, and colonies, while Germany indeed finds its way to the north, but through our independence sees its natural growth by sea cut off.
If the small beet is forced to choose a foreign language, then there is a constant alternation, also to defend our national independence.

The main question that comes up here is now only whether there is an absolute authority that not only sets rights and laws for the individual person, but also for all forms of human association, and furthermore has a means of power to maintain itself, if necessary, by the destruction of those among men who resist this authority, i.e., by deprivation of liberty, personal violation, and if necessary, by the application of the death penalty.
Nor can men by agreement give anyone the right to deprive a citizen of his liberty or take away his property.

The authority to establish laws for a people without any higher authority over it, to maintain that law even at the cost of infringing on personal freedom, personal property, and even life, and to risk the lives of citizens in war or rebellion, belongs in principle to God alone and can on earth be exercised only in His name.

To resist and oppose this, to punish by deprivation of liberty or bodily torment, and if necessary, to attack life—whether by means of the death penalty, or by suppressing rebellion, or in the case of war.
True personality only emerges where good harmony arises between the drive for independence of the individual and the drive for community in sociability.

This governmental authority is an instrument of the authority of God, and therefore has no authority above it on earth, can make laws, demand the necessary funds and services, and if necessary dispose of freedom and life.

And even now, though in a different guise, as the doctrine of the all-powerful State is again gaining ground, we are once more facing the very serious danger that, under the fair-sounding name of state sovereignty, ever more encroachment is being made on our personal and social freedom.
267 freedom of movement, and provision for the needs that are indispensable for the success of its research.

However varied the means may be that the government holds in its hands to limit the freedom of science in the exercise of its undisputed sovereign rights, indeed, almost to nullify it.

269 In the very expression “singer or painter ‘by the Grace of God’” the “sovereignty in its own sphere” is so sharply and strictly expressed that no serious writer has ever doubted that the service of Beauty could ever be anything other than a service in full awareness of absolute independence.

As soon as the Church appears publicly, requires buildings, institutions and so much more, and above all must be able to dispose of very important means, the authorities always have it in their power to do more or less serious damage to the full freedom of the Churches.

So it always comes back to our first principle, that God as Creator has established the law for every domain of human life, with Divine Sovereignty; that the constant aim of all our efforts must be to assert and maintain the laws established by God on each of these domains; and that the Government is the bearer, not of an original, but of a sovereignty resting in God and derived from God, and indeed of a sovereignty that in no way extends over our whole life, but exclusively over the magisterial relations between the inhabitants with each other and with the other peoples or colonies; and therefore it shows the characteristic distinction from all “sovereignty in its own sphere” in that it makes laws, demands submission to its laws, demands payment, if necessary deprives of freedom, and finally disposes of life and death through the death penalty and through war.
The Rights of Freedom.

That need arose through the death on May 19, 1876, of the Founder of our party, Master Guillaume Groen van Prinsterer.

As long as Groen van Prinsterer remained with us, he was, as the incarnation of the political existence and intention of the Antirevolutionary Party, the natural and life-giving center for our entire group.

But when he was taken from us, that group, lacking a bond, would have all too easily fallen apart, and in order to hold it together, Groen’s life-thoughts necessarily had to be translated into a programmatic formula and thereby interpreted for a wider circle.

Yet it was clear to me from the outset that, given a conceivable further development of the significance of our party in the Dutch political life, a more complete and more systematic exposition of what the Antirevolutionary party professed and aimed for in this country could not be avoided.
[Those who] had studied jurisprudence, but could certainly take deep glances into human life.

Not as if, since Rudolph Gneist advanced the concept of the “Rule of Law State” (*Rechtstaat*), the life of the state could be confined within the legal sphere—and even though criticism of this representation remains a duty—yet, whoever on his part wishes to give direction to the political life among his own people cannot escape the demand to explain the meaning and significance of Law.

If the scientific thought made possible thereby finds data for the Law in human nature; if history gives further definition to these natural data; and if philosophy succeeds in placing these further defined data in such a connection with the entire manifestation of life, and with our knowledge thereof, that the whole makes the impression of a well-integrated structure, or higher still, of a purely functioning organism—then one believes to have found herein that which compensates for the lack of faith, i.e., a higher power which indicates what is right or wrong, and from which the ruler of the people derives the right to subject the people to himself through coercion and the threat of punishment.

It is true that Most and Reijndorf in particular, and likewise the Nihilists in 1878, called into being a highly dangerous organization, which raged for a moment, especially in Chicago; but all organization from the Anarchist or Nihilist side was always intended exclusively as an antidote, meant to undo all existing organization of political and social life.
