Hence, in the visions that the Revelation of John opens up to us, the high song of honor for the King of Kings goes forth not from States, but “from all peoples and nations and tongues.” Not in the States, therefore, does the organic character of the community manifest itself, but rather it does so in the peoples.

So the King of Belgium in a proclamation will address the combined inhabitants of his country, or if one wishes, his combined subjects, as “my people” or “mon peuple,” even though it is also firmly established that the Belgians are of two different origins.

The fundamental principle must therefore always be held fast, as sung in one of the Evangelical hymns: “Thou, O Sovereign Ruler, hast appointed the place for every one’s abode, the circle wherein he shall work.” The influence of such a region on our life, and on the development of our own character, is therefore not accidental, but a revelation of God’s ordinance, and calls above all for appreciation and reverence.
To want to see a sovereign state in tiny enclaves like Monaco or Liechtenstein is an irony that makes a laughingstock of itself.

Yet the anxious fear constantly arises in the Austrian monarchy that the death of the Emperor, who held it all together through his personal excellence, could suddenly cause the entire empire to fall apart, especially after the atrocity in Sarajevo.

And now the fact is that it has been established otherwise, and that on the contrary the bond “that binds the heart, from mother to child” is interwoven with life, was neither our provision, nor our invention, nor even our desire or lust, but God’s sovereign decree, His will’s expression and His act.

It was Louis Napoleon who, in his decree of June 5, 1806, first presented himself here in the land as “by the Grace of God and the constitutional laws of the State, King of Holland.” The sovereign Prince did the same in his decision of December 6, 1813, the title of which read: “We, William, by the Grace of God, Prince of Orange-Nassau, Sovereign Prince of the United Netherlands, etc., etc.” However, this by no means establishes that the Princes and the common governmental administrations did not recognize the sovereignty of God’s authority as the sole source from which their high authority flowed.

The fact that this is more clearly evident in a Prince or Sovereign, since the lifelong exercise of it has more firmness and often continues genealogically in the family, has been the reason why the divine origin of supreme authority has been more felt in a Sovereign, and why the Sovereign has therefore found more occasion in a Commonwealth government than in a popular administration to abuse it for his own glorification.

The urge to do so is so overpowering that more than once a Minister of the Crown, of whom everyone knew that he had allowed the sense of atheism to dull his religious feeling, yet felt that he could not and dared not omit it, while the Bearers of the Crown were themselves so strongly convinced of this, that in all the years of our kingship not a single legislative proposal has ever been submitted to the States-General otherwise than with this final formula: “And herewith, gentlemen, we commend you to God’s holy keeping.” And although it must be acknowledged that in 1813 the obligation to include the formula “By the grace of God” in the title of the law was omitted, the fact remains nevertheless that even from the 6th of December 1813, the decree of the Sovereign Prince began with these words: “We, William, by the grace of God, Prince of Orange-Nassau, Sovereign Prince of the United Netherlands,” etc., etc., and that since 1813 not a single decree or law has ever come into force, or the same derivation of the Supreme Authority has been repeated therein, and this under the countersignature of all ministers without distinction, who thus by their co-signature also took this part of the content upon their own account.

In Jeremiah 27:5, Jehovah declares that He, as the Creator of land, men and cattle, gives the lands and kingdoms to whomever He pleases: “I have made the earth, the men and the beasts that are on the face of the earth, by My great power and by My outstretched arm, and I give it to whomever it seems right to Me.” And immediately following this general statement, it says: “And now I have given all these lands into the hand of Nebuchadnezzar the king of Babylon, My servant.” Over three generations, he, his son, and his son’s son would rule these lands.

THE INVIOLABILITY OF SUPREME AUTHORITY.245 They will remain sheep, but after that “mighty peoples and mighty kings will serve him.” Likewise, it is testified in Daniel II:21: “He deposes kings and He establishes kings,” and in IV:25: “The Most High has dominion over the kingdoms of men and gives them to whomever He will.” To Belshazzar it was said in Daniel V:18: “As for you, O king, the Most High God gave Nebuchadnezzar your father kingship and greatness and glory and majesty,” and in verse 21 the acknowledgment is demanded: “that the Most High God is ruler over the kingdoms of men, and sets over them whomever He will.” Yes, even of “the Son of Man” it is testified in Daniel VII:14 that “all peoples, nations, and languages should serve him,” just as Christ Himself declared in Matthew XXVIII:18: “All authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me.” More concretely still is what the Apostle Paul impresses upon the converted inhabitants of the capital of the empire in Romans XIII:1 ff.: Their coming under the kingship of Christ does not relieve them of their subjection to the Emperor.

A high position of the government, which in the closing book of Holy Scripture is once more connected with Christ’s dominion, as He appears (see Revelation XIX:16) “having on his robe and on his thigh a name written, King of kings and Lord of lords.” Only once does Scripture express itself in detail about a particular heathen prince.

This is said specifically of Cyrus, King of Persia, in Isaiah XLV:1: “Thus says the Lord to his anointed, to Cyrus, whose right hand I have grasped, to subdue nations before him and to loose the belts of kings, to open doors before him that gates may not be closed.”

All that in history led to the appearance of a particular Government in a people, or to the crowning of a particular person as Sovereign, took place under God’s decree, by His guidance of events, and by the provision of the person whom He Himself, God, had ordained and prepared for it.

But after having clearly explained this, we add yet that the hereditary monarchy confers such an extraordinary authority on a person, and sometimes for centuries on the same house, that it is highly necessary to add here continually that these Princes do not rule from their own head, but, themselves being nothing, derive their authority solely from the free grace of the Almighty.

The providential arrangement of God, which aims at this, is then expressed in the formula: By the Grace of God; a formula which in itself applies to all human life and all authority in this life, but has come into particular use with the monarchy, inasmuch as here the highest authority regularly passes from person to person by birth or kinship, and yet these persons do not possess this authority in their own right, but have received it solely by the gracious disposition of God.
If this is the only true Sovereignty, it follows that God alone is Sovereign, and that no human being can ever be sovereign.

This finds its cause in the fact that he who erects his legal structure with no other data than those derived from human nature, human

Conversely, he who honors the source of all right in God’s decree cannot further analyze this general conviction of faith without also, on his part, reckoning with the nature of man, with the historical course of humanity, and with the philosophical synthesis of the distinct elements of our knowledge.

The attempt to locate the source of Right in man—that is, in man’s nature, in the historical development of humanity, and in man’s philosophical, comprehensive thought—an attempt which, since the Renaissance, broke the continuity of the medieval conception of Right and became increasingly dominant in the so-called “modern” development of jurisprudence, is to be explained by nothing other than a self-deception that constantly shifts the problem.

Especially those who allowed themselves to be carried away by the Idea of Evolution in the previous century, believed they could suffice by employing three sorts of data—which they found in man’s nature, history, and philosophy—as the granite and hard stone for their construction of law, and further imagined they could dispense with all closer investigation concerning the quarry from which these blocks were hewn.

What one allows on this standpoint is exclusively threefold: first the intermediary of our natural data, second the common inspiration, and third life experience.

We cannot therefore desist from our fundamental proposition that such an essential as well as intellectual degradation of our human nature can be restored not by ordinary growth, but only by medication and operation.

That we can sometimes err in this is known to everyone from their own self-deception, but the faculty itself is in us, it belongs to the natural properties of our being “as man.” This all awakens in later life from the child’s consciousness.
Whoever strikes someone in whom that feeling sleeps or has worn out, injures the human nature of that person.

Now that this Common Grace does not fall under the Special Revelation, the whole legal administration, which at least in private law appeared in such a refined form among all nations outside Israel, and especially in the mighty Roman state, must find its explanation in natural human life; and not in the given data of a special character, of which the Holy Scripture gives us the picture.
Not from the Bible, but from nature, history, and the science of the peoples outside Israel, must this enthronement of the law be known.

But although, with a view to this establishment of a natural legal administration, which is indeed supported by Common Grace, but nevertheless took place outside the Holy Scripture, special emphasis should be placed on the sense of the legal sensation, which is inseparable from our nature, this does not in any way mean that this pre-Christian legal administration took place outside all Revelation.

Now where, in that first-mentioned period of over two thousand years, do we find any special revelation that would guide us in the legal inheritance of God’s ways?

The special legal revelation does not appear in a world part that is still entirely uncultivated, in order to create entirely new conditions there, but on the contrary, it emerges in the center of the world movement of that time, at the focal point where the lines radiating from Babylon, Persia, Egypt, Greece, and soon from Rome, intersect.

The special Revelation does not first begin to embroider the figures of law upon the warp of the life of the nations, but finds among all nations customary laws and often even written laws, called upon to order social and political life.
Rather, one must begin by investigating the significance of the legal sphere into which the special Revelation has inserted itself.
Whatever the special Revelation offers, however sparkling new in origin and source, it always weaves further upon the garment that the Creation has wrapped around us.
What this Revelation bestows upon us always connects to the existing, and therefore the starting point for the knowledge of the whole can never lie elsewhere than in the existing.
How, then, apart from the dispensation of Special Revelation, has a certain, very many-sided, yet always elevating legal regulation come into being on so broad a scale?

Now that this Common Grace does not fall under the Special Revelation, the whole legal administration, which at least in private law appeared in such a refined form among all nations outside Israel, and especially in the mighty Roman state, must find its explanation in natural human life; and not in the given data of a special character, of which the Holy Scripture gives us the picture.

But although, with a view to this establishment of a natural legal administration, which is indeed supported by Common Grace, but nevertheless took place outside the Holy Scripture, special emphasis should be placed on the sense of the legal sensation, which is inseparable from our nature, this does not in any way mean that this pre-Christian legal administration took place outside all Revelation.
Of course, one must be on one’s guard in considering this, so as not to blur or erase the boundary line that separates the general from the special Revelation.

Time and again, history tells us of extraordinary personalities and extraordinary events that brought about a mighty turning point in existence; and in all this too there is revelation; but always in such a way and to such a degree that the personal and the natural are not transcended, and that there is no question of a Special Revelation.

In the appearance of extraordinary personalities in the true scientific and political domain, there is nothing that transcends the natural, nor are the boundaries of common revelation overstepped, if a concurrence of extraordinary events brings about a change in the situation.
The distinction between common and special Revelation is by no means abolished by these exceptional appearances and phenomena.
Never, on the other hand, should the notion be admitted that God’s decrees and ordinances, and thus also God’s Revelation, are to be discerned only in the particular, but not in the natural.

REVOLUTION.excerpt: It is therefore nothing other than the contact with life, which still rustled in the so-called Night School, through which I recognized the impossibility, on the one hand, of separating theology and politics from one another, and, on the other hand, of choosing 1789 instead of 1578 as the starting point for the historical-political trajectory.

Let us not forget that even before Darwin, when the theory of evolution had not yet come, the less far-reaching representation was already in vogue, as if man, having come into the world as a half-barbarian, had only gradually become civilized and humane, something which in the days of the French Revolution was again disputed by reference to the inhabitants of the South Sea islands and by the raving about the state of innocence of nature—something that especially drove the Aboriginal Society in London, but these small variations need not be noted here.
Against this, human self-awareness has finally risen in revolt.

Thomas Jefferson and his followers undoubtedly leaned in that direction, but Alexander Hamilton resisted him and did not hesitate to testify “that the Revolution in America had as little in common with the revolution in Paris as the adulterous woman in a French novel with the Puritan matron in New England.” Hamilton’s leading thought remained the founding of a Christian Constitutional popular group.

This tendency had already emerged long before Rousseau, but it was particularly the French Revolution that preached, in ever-widening circles, the doctrine that all peoples had to conform to a fixed model, and that only in this way could the long-promised and long-awaited state of bliss be attained.

And precisely for this reason we appear as Anti-Revolutionaries, not at all to deny that in 1789 and in the years that followed, much went to the grave that had already dragged on its shameful existence for too long, but much rather to be grateful that the storm that then arose also brought a purification for our country that awakened, refreshed and strengthened many a dormant vital force in our circle.

A new spirit of life has animated us, solely in order to ward off, with full acknowledgment of this gain, the unchangeable and unyielding curse that the Revolution brought upon our human life when it severed the bond that also connected our state life to the Almighty, and subjected us to the tyranny of the human spirit worshiping itself as God.

Even in the Marseillaise, the mightiest battle song of the French Revolution, which actually aimed at the international abolition of all national life, the “Allons, enfants de la patrie” has been the expression of the sweeping inspiration that made the stream of the people surge high.

Right, taken in this sense—as a regulation of relationships established by God, in which conscious creatures ought to stand and interact with Him and mutually with one another—is thus a disposition proceeding entirely independently from God alone, the knowledge of which can only come to us out of or through Him.

However much here, for our purpose, the renewal of the lost knowledge of the law comes to the fore, yet it needs no further argument that with the knowledge of the law, an imprinting in the memory is not enough.
Legal knowledge without restored legal consciousness may enrich the memory—deepen and clarify it—but it cannot elevate your legal awareness.
Here too the assertion returns that it is God who reveals to us the lost knowledge of the law, but on the condition that this happens exclusively in a completely natural way.

The regeneration of each individual person, and the Incarnation of the Son of God for our entire race, combined with prophecy and direct revelation, remain for us the indispensable extraordinary data that can lead us to the knowledge of the law as it is in God and comes from God over us.
Yet he is completely mistaken who, placing himself on this standpoint, imagines that we have nothing else to do in order to arrive at the knowledge of the law than to open up the Holy Scriptures.
What this Revelation bestows upon us always connects to the existing, and therefore the starting point for the knowledge of the whole can never lie elsewhere than in the existing.

Quite apart from the question whether there is an innate legal knowledge in us, it is the sense of justice implanted in us and maintained against all corruption, which has made an ordered society among men possible, and it cannot be praised highly enough that the danger that arose from the Fall against this has been averted by Common Grace.
Not from the Bible, but from nature, history, and the science of the peoples outside Israel, must this enthronement of the law be known.

The knowledge of the right can be thought of: 1° as innate to us by nature; 2° as communicated to us, i.e., by word, dream, or image; and 3° as something for us to deduce from the data that nature and history set before us.
Only where the spiritual sense for the perception of right is present and active can knowledge of the right be acquired.
Wisdom in distinction from intellectual knowledge, tact in conduct, the ability to make a fortunate grasp, even genius—all these are akin to the instinctive.
The assertion is therefore not valid that the instinct itself brings us knowledge of rights.

The dove does not arrive at this knowledge through reasoning, nor through what it has learned in ornithology, for even if it has never before seen such a bird of prey, it still knows instinctively that it is dealing with its enemy.
There is a knowledge of right and wrong that instinctively arises from life itself.

Our conclusion can therefore be no other than that before all positive law, before all legal research, and before all science of law, there were always present in human nature certain purely instinctive legal sensations, from which our innate sense of legal sensation immediately grasped the distinction between what is right and what is wrong.
We must now investigate, in the second place, to what extent we have been granted conscious knowledge of the law from God’s side, whether through the Word, or also through dream or vision.
What is naturally our own works instinctively, and emerges first from this instinctive [knowledge] into conscious knowledge.
Now the only knowledge available to us on this point is that which is offered to us in the oldest historical records that have been handed down to us in Holy Scripture.

Certainly, the capacity for this sensation has also been diminished, but not nearly as much as our sensitivity in the aesthetic or moral sphere.

Among entire peoples, the pure sensation in the sexual sphere may have become as worn out and extinct as among the Italians the sensitivity in the struggle of the true against the lie may have become evident, but wherever you move among the peoples, the sensation of right or wrong still vibrates under all nations, sometimes far too sharply rather than too weakly, and it still expresses itself everywhere with the spontaneous resilience that makes you think more of overstimulation than of wear and tear of the sense of justice, here of course understood purely formally.
It is the same with the sensation for the beautiful in the world of tones.

SIN.excerpt: Hence, in the Holy Scripture, matters that, once detached from the bond of Right, diverge into morality and piety, could fall under the single concept of Right—a distinction that then encompasses under morality all that pertains to our relationship to ourselves and our fellow creatures, and honors as piety our correct relationship to God.
To willfully and knowingly allow fruit to rot in the orchard or on the field is sin, not because the law does not permit it, but because the moral sense condemns it.

Precisely as Christ articulated the love for God: “With all your soul, with all your heart, with all your understanding, and with all your strength.” This therefore amounts to the fact that, sin excluded, the intention of the moral motive would cover the entire terrain of complete righteousness.
Now, however, sin crept in, and the intention of the individual will not only lost conformity with God’s will but even aroused an impulse to go against God’s will.
Only, that remaining terrain does not fall under the administration of justice maintained by the Magistracy.

Over that broad terrain, the administration of justice remains with God—partially now already in the conscience and in the providential connection of suffering to sin—but to come into full scope only at the Last Judgment, insofar as it has not already found its completion through the Justification that faith brings.

And what is no less worthy of note: the absence of the sinful element, due to the non-occurrence of the Fall in Paradise, would by no means have excluded a gradual growth through education and development, and an ascent in the degree of both the consciousness of right and the consciousness of morality.

The transition from the unconsciousness of the newborn to the consciousness of the adult, from the primitive innocence of the youth to the maturity of the man, and from a less delicate to a finer sensibility as the fruit of life experience and culture, would, apart from sin, in no way have pointed back to an earlier unholy state.
Such a natural development would have had nothing in common with sin, in whatever sense.
Of course, this would stand out even more clearly if here too the after-effects of our fall into sin did not creep in.

But even though under many flaws and many temptations to sin, the Common Grace has also here worked to avert corruption, and has enriched us in the course of history with a culture that ever more nobly unfolds.
The obscuring by sin set in and continued.
It had to undo the consequences of the fall through personal atonement of the violated law and through the principled sanctification of the corrupted soul-life.
It had to cut off the return from the fall.

And when one descends to the sensual, one need only think of polygamy, of prostitution, and of the unnatural sin of Romans 1:26ff., of the sin of drunkenness, of the gambling mania, and so much more, to repeatedly be appalled at the laxity of morals that, if not approving of all this, at least turns a blind eye to it.
But it is precisely in connection with this that it is so remarkably the case that the sensation of right or wrong has comparatively lost so much of its edge since our fall into sin.

As soon, however, as reverence for the law began to suffer from the violation of sin, the resistance to this went forth on the basis of a threefold personal, religious and social motive: 1° from our innate sense of justice, 2° from the awareness that the law is of God, so that its violation was an offense against God, and 3° strengthened by the echo that our own cry of distress met with among others.

However, the very fact that this reaction could only arise once injustice began to take place, that it was unthinkable before the Fall, and could only express itself in a sinful generation, naturally brought about the fact that from the outset in the reaction of the sense of justice against injustice, the fallen state of man had to be reckoned with, and consequently the purity, the holiness and the unanimity of the emerging reaction could be lost.

Quite apart from the question whether there is an innate legal knowledge in us, it is the sense of justice implanted in us and maintained against all corruption, which has made an ordered society among men possible, and it cannot be praised highly enough that the danger that arose from the Fall against this has been averted by Common Grace.

Now that this Common Grace does not fall under the Special Revelation, the whole legal administration, which at least in private law appeared in such a refined form among all nations outside Israel, and especially in the mighty Roman state, must find its explanation in natural human life; and not in the given data of a special character, of which the Holy Scripture gives us the picture.
The special Revelation gave life, correction and supplement, but among the peoples outside of Israel and as an outflow of Creation, Fall and Common Grace, the edifice of law has arisen.

If the ability to undergo a sensation of right or wrong is innate in human nature, and the Special Revelation only provides confirmation, correction and supplement for the Right, then two possibilities present themselves for the content of the Right, namely, either that it is a fiction of human play of chance, or that it has presented itself to the bare formal sense of Right in humans from God’s side, and has been accepted as imposed by God; something in which one must always reckon with the natural circumstance that wrong only stands opposed to Right after the Fall.

other than that the satisfaction of God’s right on earth must be the starting point for all Christian faith, and that the sliding over the violation of God’s right, which constitutes the character of all sin, to seek a way out in pious pretense, not only falsifies religion, and especially the Christian religion, in its essence, but also deprives all legal administration on earth of its firm foundation in God Himself.

One knows the theories that dissolve and undermine the right, which in the transgressors only want to see a victim of his corrupting environment, and which condemn all punishment as a transgression against the unfortunate.

One might even be inclined to regard the instinct in man as little more than a crutch or leading-string to teach him to walk, which would then also fall away of itself once he had finally learned to walk.

If a young child crawls onto a landing that leads out onto a dangerous staircase, and someone comes along and sees that the child will fall down from there onto the marble floor below, you cannot just walk on, however busy you may be; then a magical instinct, together with a strongly felt sense of duty, impels you to pull the child away from that staircase, and if you succeed, you know exceedingly well that you have no right to boast of extraordinary kindness, but that the mother of the child had a right that you would safeguard her child from any potentially deadly fall.
For the time being, the question posed applies exclusively to human life, as it found its ordering in the creation and partly retained it after its disruption by the Fall.
According to Holy Scripture, the organization of animals underwent a significant change only after the Fall, and the mutual slaughter in the animal world has to a considerable extent become the rule.

STATE.excerpt: Thus I naturally came home to Calvin, and since Church and State were so intimately and closely connected in the 16th-century conception, my studies were already then drawn, to a certain extent, toward the political terrain.

The supremacy of the State (XII), when merely traced alongside the States Party and the Anabaptists, immediately reveals how thoroughly the Calvinism of that era developed and matured in a political sense even among the “common folk.” I sought to satisfy the need to reawaken this same striving and intention—and to guide it where it still proved awake—first in *Our Program* and later in *Common Grace*.

Not as if, since Rudolph Gneist advanced the concept of the “Rule of Law State” (*Rechtstaat*), the life of the state could be confined within the legal sphere—and even though criticism of this representation remains a duty—yet, whoever on his part wishes to give direction to the political life among his own people cannot escape the demand to explain the meaning and significance of Law.

Both directions—that of Anarchism and that of Russian Nihilism—thus resulted in the downfall of all communal life in both society and state; but it must be recognized that both logically mapped out their course, by carrying the denial of the source of law in God through to the ultimate consequence.

Those, however, who shrank back from the logical consequence of their similar starting point, and maintained the communal life of men—in families and society, in Church and State—as necessary, were unable to maintain the human origin of law otherwise than by leaving deep thought to others, and merely delighting themselves in gazing upon the water’s surface.

The transition from the unconsciousness of the newborn to the consciousness of the adult, from the primitive innocence of the youth to the maturity of the man, and from a less delicate to a finer sensibility as the fruit of life experience and culture, would, apart from sin, in no way have pointed back to an earlier unholy state.

Now that this Common Grace does not fall under the Special Revelation, the whole legal administration, which at least in private law appeared in such a refined form among all nations outside Israel, and especially in the mighty Roman state, must find its explanation in natural human life; and not in the given data of a special character, of which the Holy Scripture gives us the picture.

Let us not forget that even before Darwin, when the theory of evolution had not yet come, the less far-reaching representation was already in vogue, as if man, having come into the world as a half-barbarian, had only gradually become civilized and humane, something which in the days of the French Revolution was again disputed by reference to the inhabitants of the South Sea islands and by the raving about the state of innocence of nature—something that especially drove the Aboriginal Society in London, but these small variations need not be noted here.

To do what has long been done in England, and also earlier under Rome in the latifundia, namely to withdraw entire regions that are intended for the supply of food for men and animals from this destination and to close them off as hunting grounds, goes against God’s ordinance, and it is a revival of the sense of justice that one is now making in England and Scotland to put an end to this monstrous state of affairs.

In the disordered state in which we find ourselves after the fall, the purity of this distinction may have suffered damage in not a few individuals, so that we now repeatedly stumble upon effeminate men and mannish women, but this does not take away the fact that the masculine and the feminine type are essentially distinct and that they still present themselves as unequal in almost every respect in the vast majority of persons.
This may frequently occur in our weakened state, but it is not the natural order.

Quite spontaneously, the family emerges in this way, and as long as one has not been seduced by fantasies, such as Plato advocated as the highest wisdom, the family formation governs the whole of society, and thus, in a normal state of affairs, the legal relationship between man and woman, parents and children, brothers and sisters, free and servants, household members and strangers regulates itself.
In an innocent, paradisiacal state, the instinctive and innate sense of right would have provided all the necessary guidance on every point of life with perfect purity and clarity.

Now, on the other hand, since we have passed from that pure state into such an unhealthy state, it is entirely natural that the legal relationship is by no means always and for everyone seen with sufficient clarity, as soon as the demand of the substance of life is either not sufficiently understood or is falsified against better knowledge.
